text: Here we discuss our method of excising difficult-to-remove gastric cysts or gastrointestinal stromal tumors. These tumors are often located on the lesser curve, posterior wall, and oricardia of the stomach. They can be endo or exophytic. Robotic surgery provides an ergonomic benefit over standard laparoscopy to access these types of GISTs because they are not amenable to stapled wedge resection.

Here we demonstrate our technique for robotic resection of an endophytic posterior gastric GIST located at the cardia. We have no disclosures. Here we show representative CT slices of a patient who presented with a posterior wall GIST in the cardia of the stomach. We will demonstrate how a tumor such as this can be removed using a robotic approach.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative EGD to confirm the intragastric tumor location, followed by careful dissection along the lesser curve to mobilize the stomach. A gastrotomy is made, and the tumor is stapled beneath the stalk. The gastrotomy site is closed with an imbricated two-layer closure, and a leak test is conducted under direct visualization with a repeat EGD to ensure no leakage.

The surgical team demonstrates the port placements for the robotic procedure, showing five ports including a bedside assistant port. An additional liver retractor is visible. The robotic arms are equipped with a fenestrated bipolar grasper, a laparoscopic robotic camera, an alternating electrocautery hook, vessel sealer, or robotic stapler, and caudate forceps.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative EGD to confirm the gastrotomy site placement, revealing the tumor within the gastric lumen. Using hook electrocautery, the gastrotomy site is created, and the laparoscopic camera is inserted to visualize the intragastric tumor. The gastrotomy site is then enlarged to facilitate the elevation and stapled resection of the tumor.

The surgeon enlarges the gastrotomy site near the lesser curve vasculature to mobilize the gastric GIST. The tumor is elevated, allowing the stapler to pass underneath its stalk, ensuring negative margins for pathology.

The surgeon closes the gastrotomy site using a horizontal running V-lock suture, followed by a second imbricated layer with a vertical running V-lock suture. After closure, an intraoperative EGD is performed to examine the suture layers from inside the stomach, demonstrating a negative leak test.

The excised posterior wall gastric gist is displayed. The surgical process involved confirming the tumor location with an EGD, careful stomach dissection for tumor mobilization, stapling below the tumor base for R-zero resection, and closing the gastrotomy site with an imbricated two-layer closure followed by a negative leak test.
